---
title: "Output Validation"
subtitle: "Cross-validation tests, R package lockfile, and thread-count determinism"
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
---

These tools validate that pipeline output is correct, reproducible, and
concordant between the DB path and legacy path. Cross-validation tests compare
the two assessment paths. The R package lockfile and GCTA thread-pinning
strategy ensure reproducibility across runs and environments.

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '16px'}}}%%
flowchart LR
    classDef val fill:#e07b3a,stroke:#a3572a,color:#fff
    classDef data fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#999,color:#333
    classDef ok fill:#48b96e,stroke:#2d8150,color:#fff

    DB(["db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv"]):::data
    LEG(["simulation_assessment_results.tsv"]):::data
    LOCK(["renv.lock"]):::data

    CV1["test-assessment_cross_validation.R"]:::val
    CV2["test-cross_validation.R"]:::val
    THR["Thread-count determinism"]:::val
    LCK["renv::restore lockfile verification"]:::val

    PASS(["Validated output"]):::ok

    DB & LEG --> CV1 --> PASS
    DB --> CV2 --> PASS
    THR --> PASS
    LOCK --> LCK --> PASS
```


## Cross-Validation Tests

Three integration test files are the primary evaluation metric for pipeline
correctness. Together they cover database structure, data round-trip fidelity,
and assessment concordance between the DB and legacy paths.

**`test-db_structure.R`** (27 tests, requires `TEST_DB_DIR`) — verifies the
Parquet database written by `DB_MIGRATION_*` modules: directory structure,
Parquet schemas for all six artifact types (marker sets, genotypes, traits,
causal variants, phenotypes, mappings), metadata completeness, DuckDB
queryability, and two invariants established by issue111:

- Inbred `meta$n_markers == marker_set_metadata$n_markers` (GCTA processes all
  markers for fastGWA-mlm-exact)
- Loco `meta$n_markers < marker_set_metadata$n_markers` (GCTA silently excludes
  near-singular markers from mlma-loco output)

**`test-assessment_cross_validation.R`** (10 tests, requires
`TEST_LEGACY_ASSESSMENT` and `TEST_DB_ASSESSMENT`) — compares
`db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv` (DB path) against
`simulation_assessment_results.tsv` (legacy path). Tests: output column
completeness (all 35 `format_assessment_tsv()` columns), `calculate_simrep_performance()`
runs correctly with Power and FDR in [0, 1], `designate_qtl()` produces only
the 4 valid outcome categories, plus three concordance scores per replicate
(detection, designation, interval Jaccard). Requires `--legacy_assess` when
collecting test data.

**`test-cross_validation.R`** (8 tests, requires `TEST_DB_DIR` and
`TEST_WORK_DIR`) — compares DB Parquet contents against raw `.fastGWA`/`.mlma`
files in the Nextflow work directory. Tests: P/BETA/SE/AF1 round-trip to
1e-12 tolerance, `read_raw_gwa_file()` normalized column schema for both
formats, `query_by_mapping_id()` coverage for every mapping ID in metadata,
EIGEN threshold consistency, and the loco < inbred marker count invariant as a
regression guard for issue111.

All three tests skip automatically unless all required env vars are set.

### CV Pool Scenario: No Legacy Cross-Validation Path

When testing the `test_cv_pool` profile (`cv_maf=0.01 < ms_maf=0.05`), the legacy
path is incompatible and **`test-assessment_cross_validation.R` must not be run**.
Non-marker causal variants (MAF between 0.01 and 0.05) are entirely absent from GWA
output files and therefore invisible to `Get_GCTA_Intervals.R` and `Assess_Sims.R`.
A legacy assessment TSV for this profile would silently misrepresent False Negative
rates by omitting these variants from scoring.

The standalone `test-cv_pool.R` (7 tests, requires `TEST_CV_POOL=true`) provides
DB-path-only quality checks for this scenario:

- Confirms that non-marker causal variants appear as `Simulated=TRUE, log10p=NA` FN
  rows in the DB assessment output
- Validates `Simulated.QTL.VarExp` is populated for these rows via the per-trait
  causal genotype path (`traits/causal_genotypes/`)
- Asserts `significant=NA` (not `FALSE`) for non-marker rows — distinguishing "absent
  from GWA" from "tested but below threshold"
- Verifies `designate_qtl()` classifies `significant=NA` FN rows as `Missed.CV` so
  they contribute to the Power denominator in `calculate_simrep_performance()`
- Confirms Power < 1 for at least one replicate (non-marker causal variants are
  structurally undetectable, reducing achievable power)

::: {.callout-note}
## Power interpretation for cv_pool runs

When `cv_maf < ms_maf`, the maximum achievable Power is less than 1 because some
simulated causal variants are invisible to GWA by design. This is the intended
behaviour for studying the effect of rare causal variants below the GWA MAF cutoff —
it is not a pipeline bug. The `test-cv_pool.R` assertion `Power < 1` validates this
scenario is being exercised.
:::


## R Package Lockfile

`renv.lock` at the project root records the exact versions of all R packages
installed in the `andersenlab/r_packages:20250519` container. This file is a
reproducibility audit artifact — it lets you verify which package versions
produced a given set of simulation results and reproduce the environment
locally if needed.

### What is covered

The lockfile covers packages used by `R_.*` Nextflow processes (`bin/` scripts):

| Script | Key packages |
|--------|-------------|
| `bin/Get_GenoMatrix_Eigen.R` | Rcpp, dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, stringr, readr, glue, purrr, coop |
| `bin/Create_Causal_QTLs.R` | dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, stringr, readr, IRanges, fuzzyjoin, glue, purrr |
| `bin/Get_GCTA_Intervals.R` | dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, stringr, readr, glue, purrr, ggbeeswarm |
| `bin/Assess_Sims.R` | dplyr, tidyr, data.table, GenomicRanges, purrr, glue |

`DB_MIGRATION_.*` processes run in a separate container
(`andersenlab/migrate-sims:20260202`) and are not covered by this lockfile.

### Reproducing the environment locally

```r
# Install renv if not present
install.packages("renv")

# Restore the exact package versions recorded in renv.lock
renv::restore(lockfile = "renv.lock")
```

### Regenerating the lockfile

Regenerate `renv.lock` whenever the `r_packages` container is rebuilt:

```bash
# Local (Docker)
bash scripts/generate_renv_lock.sh docker

# HPC / Rockfish (Singularity)
bash scripts/generate_renv_lock.sh singularity
```

The script requires an explicit runtime argument (`docker` or `singularity`).
It pulls `andersenlab/r_packages:20250519`, runs `renv::snapshot(type = "all")`
inside the container via a bind-mounted project root, and writes `renv.lock`.
Commit the updated file.


## Thread-Count Determinism

All GCTA steps except `--mlma-loco` are pinned to `--thread-num 1` to ensure
reproducible floating-point results across local and HPC runs. Multi-threaded BLAS
uses non-deterministic partial-sum reduction order — differences propagate from GRM
construction through REML, PCA, and the LMM solve into BETA, SE, and P-values.

| GCTA step | `--thread-num` | Rationale |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| `--make-grm-inbred` / `--make-grm` | **1** | Errors here propagate to all downstream steps |
| `--reml` | **1** | Governs Vp upscaling decision; must be consistent at borderline values |
| `--simu-qt` | **1** | Ensures phenotype simulation is deterministic for a given random seed |
| `--pca 1` | **1** | Eigenvector iteration sensitive to BLAS reduction order |
| `--fastGWA-mlm-exact` | **1** | LMM solve; diverges between local and HPC without pinning |
| `--mlma-loco` | **configurable (`task.cpus`)** | Per-chromosome refits are independent; highest compute cost; least numerically sensitive step |

The `gcta_make_grm` and `gcta_simulate_phenotypes` Rockfish labels are configured
with `cpus = 1` since all their GCTA operations are single-threaded. The
`gcta_perform_gwa` label retains `cpus = 4` to provide CPU headroom for
`--mlma-loco` on larger datasets.

### BLAS implementation in the production container

`gcta64` is a **static executable** — `ldd` returns "not a dynamic executable".
Inspect the binary with `strings` to identify the embedded BLAS:

```bash
docker run --rm --platform linux/amd64 \
  quay.io/biocontainers/gcta:1.94.1--h9ee0642_0 \
  sh -c 'strings $(which gcta64) | grep -i -E "mkl|openblas|goto|omp_set_num_threads"'
```

Result: **Intel oneMKL**, statically linked, with **GNU OpenMP** as the thread
runtime (`gomp_ialias_omp_set_num_threads`).

#### How MKL + OpenMP cause non-determinism in GCTA

Intel MKL parallelises dense linear algebra by splitting work across OpenMP
threads and reducing partial results at the end. Floating-point addition is not
associative — `(a + b) + c ≠ a + (b + c)` in IEEE 754 — so the order in which
thread-local partial sums are accumulated determines the final value. OpenMP does
not guarantee a fixed reduction order, and MKL actively varies its internal
tiling and accumulation strategy based on the thread count and the SIMD
instruction set available at runtime. The consequences for each GCTA operation
are:

| Operation | How threading introduces non-determinism |
|-----------|------------------------------------------|
| `--make-grm[-inbred]` | Each GRM entry is a sum of allele products over all markers. MKL threads split the marker axis; partial sums are reduced in thread-completion order, which varies run to run. |
| `--reml` | REML is an iterative EM algorithm driven by matrix-vector products on the GRM. Non-deterministic accumulation in each MV product shifts the convergence path, producing different Vp estimates and affecting the `GCTA_MAKE_GRM` iterative REML scaling decision at borderline values. |
| `--pca 1` | Power-iteration eigenvector computation applies repeated MV products. Accumulated rounding differences rotate the eigenvector slightly each run, changing the PC1 covariate value passed to the LMM. |
| `--fastGWA-mlm-exact` | The exact LMM solve applies a sparse Cholesky factorization. Thread-parallel factorization produces different fill-in and accumulated roundoff, shifting BETA, SE, and P-values for markers near the significance boundary. |
| `--mlma-loco` | Same Cholesky-based LMM, applied once per chromosome. Individual chromosome solves are independent, so parallelism across chromosomes is safe — only within-solve threading (controlled by `--thread-num`) introduces non-determinism. |

MKL also exposes `MKL_DYNAMIC`, which allows MKL to adjust its own thread count
at runtime independent of the application. With `MKL_DYNAMIC=TRUE` (the MKL
default), MKL may use fewer threads than requested if it judges the workload too
small — meaning thread count, and therefore reduction order, can differ between
runs on the same machine. Pinning `--thread-num 1` disables intra-operation
parallelism entirely, making reduction order deterministic regardless of
`MKL_DYNAMIC`.

#### Thread control hierarchy

`--thread-num 1` is the primary control and is sufficient for determinism.
`OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` and `MKL_NUM_THREADS=1` are independent env-var overrides
that remain effective if any code path bypasses the GCTA flag (e.g., a direct MKL
call from a dependency). These are noted as a future belt-and-suspenders hardening
option.

::: {.callout-tip}
## See also
For the concordance methodology and score interpretation, see
[Assessment Cross-Validation](cross-validation.qmd).
:::
